metrics.reason: Primary indicator of memory pressure; high values show frequent paging activity.
Correlates with Pages/sec; a rise indicates the system is unable to find pages in RAM and must fetch from disk.
Shows how much memory is being used for cache; a drop may force more page reads.
Low values can cause excessive paging because the OS cannot allocate page tables efficiently.
Non‑paged pool growth often precedes memory pressure; high values can starve regular allocations.
Direct measurement of paging file consumption; high usage confirms that RAM is insufficient.
CPU saturation can exacerbate paging; ensures the issue isn’t caused by CPU bottlenecks.
User‑mode CPU consumption; helps differentiate CPU‑bound vs memory‑bound load.
Kernel‑mode CPU usage; high values may indicate driver or OS activity contributing to paging.
Deferred Procedure Calls can cause spikes in memory activity; useful for root‑cause.
Interrupt handling load; excessive interrupts can increase paging pressure.
High context‑switch rate often accompanies memory pressure and can amplify paging.
Queue length > 2 per core indicates the system is waiting for CPU, which may be a side‑effect of memory thrashing.
Thread count growth can increase memory consumption; useful to see if thread explosion is causing pressure.
Overall memory utilization percentage; validates that RAM is near capacity.
Absolute amount of RAM in use; helps quantify pressure.
Total physical memory; needed to calculate utilization.
Free space in the paging file; low free space can cause high Pages/sec.
Percentage of free swap; complements swap.free metric.
Total size of the paging file; required for context on swap usage.
Disk C: space usage; low free space can affect paging file size and performance.
Disk D: space usage; same rationale as C: drive.
Disk idle time; low idle time may indicate heavy I/O caused by paging.
Average read latency; high latency can worsen paging impact.
Average write latency; important when paging file writes are frequent.
Current I/O queue depth; a growing queue often accompanies paging activity.
Network receive errors; while not directly memory‑related, they can indicate broader system stress.
Network transmit errors; same rationale as above.
Ensures the monitoring agent itself is healthy; a failing agent could produce false alerts.
Neo4j is a memory‑intensive service; its state helps determine if it contributes to pressure.
Redis can consume significant RAM; checking its state aids correlation.
